Generate a file named AGENTS.md that serves as a contributor guide for this repository.
Your goal is to produce a clear, concise, and well-structured document with descriptive headings and actionable explanations for each section.
Follow the outline below, but adapt as needed — add sections if relevant, and omit those that do not apply to this project.

Document Requirements

- Title the document "Repository Guidelines".
- Use Markdown headings (#, ##, etc.) for structure.
- Keep the document concise. 200-400 words is optimal.
- Keep explanations short, direct, and specific to this repository.
- Provide examples where helpful (commands, directory paths, naming patterns).
- Maintain a professional, instructional tone.

Recommended Sections

Project Structure & Module Organization

- Outline the project structure, including where the source code, tests, and assets are located.

Build, Test, and Development Commands

- List key commands for building, testing, and running locally (e.g., npm test, make build).
- Briefly explain what each command does.

Coding Style & Naming Conventions

- Specify indentation rules, language-specific style preferences, and naming patterns.
- Include any formatting or linting tools used.

Testing Guidelines

- Identify testing frameworks and coverage requirements.
- State test naming conventions and how to run tests.

Commit & Pull Request Guidelines

- Summarize commit message conventions found in the project’s Git history.
- Outline pull request requirements (descriptions, linked issues, screenshots, etc.).

(Optional) Add other sections if relevant, such as Security & Configuration Tips, Architecture Overview, or Agent-Specific Instructions.

# Anedot

This is the core Anedot web application code base.

## Setup

First time? Check out the [New setup checklist in the wiki](https://github.com/anedot/anedot/wiki/New-setup-checklist)

```shell
bin/setup
```

## Development

```shell
# Run the app
bundle exec rails s
bundle exec sidekiq
bin/webpack-dev-server

# Migrate the database
bundle exec rails db:migrate

# Reset the database
bundle exec rails db:reset

# Database related commands (postgresql/redis)
brew services start postgresql   # start and register to launch on login
brew services stop postgresql
brew services restart postgresql
brew services run postgresql     # start without launching on login
```

http://anedot.me:3000

## Tests
`bundle exec rspec`

## Linter
`bundle exec rubocop`

## Database Consistency
`bundle exec rake database_consistency`

## Regenerate Swagger
`RAILS_ENV=test bundle exec rake rswag:specs:swaggerize`

## API docs
`http://anedot.me:3000/api-docs/index.html`

# Pull Request Guidelines

## Title

We want the title of the pull request formatted in such a way that it helps determine what was merged when viewing from git logs.

The general format is: `[CODE] SHORT_DESCRIPTION`

**Examples:**

- [BUGFIX] Donation was saving data to the wrong table
- [FEATURE] Account filter UI revisions
- [INTERNAL] Refactor Donation model for code quality
- [MAINTENANCE] Update gems

### Code

A value from the list below that generally describes what was done.

- `BUGFIX` - _fix for an outstanding bug that should have a corresponding Issue._
- `FEATURE` - _user facing change that should have a corresponding Issue._
- `INTERNAL` - _internal changes with no user facing elements, like refactoring or moving code around, that should have a corresponding Issue._
- `MAINTENANCE` - _application/infrastructure maintenance like gem/library updates, may or may not have an Issue._
- `SECURITY` - _application/infrastructure updates that directly affect security, should have a corresponding Issue._

### Short Description

A short description of the pull request contents. It should allow the entire pull request title to fit in approx. 80 characters.

## Description

The detailed description of the pull request. It helps the team understand what is being delivered and where to focus their attention during the code review.

- Describe what the pull request is addressing
- Are there any known issues with the pull request as is?
- Additional Issue numbers if needed
- Add screenshots to help quickly understand UI changes if they are present

Make sure to ping the appropriate individuals or teams for a code review.

## Commit Messages

Adhere to the style conventions in the canonical reference from Tim Pope:

- [A Note About Git Commit Messages](http://tbaggery.com/2008/04/19/a-note-about-git-commit-messages.html)

More context may be found here:

- [How to Write a Git Commit Message](http://chris.beams.io/posts/git-commit/)

Please take the time to read, internalize, and follow these guidelines.

## Labels

Use labels to clarify PR status.

- needs review(s)
- depends on PR
- qa needed

Make sure to add the _"needs { } review"_ label to indicate when another developer is needed for an interim or final review.

For drafts or "work in progress", use the Draft feature to indicate that the code is not complete and not ready to merge.

See QA section for QA-related labels.

## Reviewing

Use GitHub's Reviewers functionality to add yourself to a Pull Request when reviewing a Pull Request. Do not remove the respective _"needs { } review"_ label until you complete your review.

## QA

### Requesting QA

If your Pull Request is in need of QA, please add _"needs QA"_ label.

### Performing QA

If you are QA'ing a Pull Request, please wait until all _"needs { } review"_ labels are removed, unless specifically requested.

1. Add yourself as a reviewer.

2. If you need to deploy it to a QA environment or Sandbox, add one of the following labels:

- qa1
- qa2
- qa3
- sandbox

The Anedot bot will deploy to QA (or Sandbox) based on the label you use. The bot will also update the labels to indicate deployment status.

3. Remove the _needs QA_ and _qa{}/sandbox_ labels and add `🏁 qa complete` once complete.

## Merging

Please wait until at least one other developer has given approval before merging. Please wait for explicit approval, such as a comment like "I approve of this PR" or "LGTM" _(Looks Good To Me)_. Use of happy Emoji paired with the approval comments is encouraged.

**Important:** Make sure all branches are rebased on top of the latest `master` before merging to keep history clear and avoid "merge bubbles".

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: AGENTS.md
+# Repository Guidelines
+
+## Project Structure & Module Organization
+- Rails app with business logic in `app/controllers`, `app/models`, `app/services`, `app/workers`, and `app/views`; background jobs via Sidekiq.
+- Frontend lives in `app/frontend` (Webpacker): entry packs in `app/frontend/packs`, shared components in `app/frontend/components`, namespaces for feature slices, and supporting libs in `app/frontend/lib`.
+- Tests: Ruby specs in `spec` (models, requests, services, etc.); JS/React tests run from `app/frontend` and `jest` (mocks/setup) with coverage output to `coverage/`.
+- Configuration in `config/` (environment files, initializers, routes); database schema and migrations in `db/`; static assets and compiled outputs in `public/`.
+
+## Setup, Build, and Run
+- First time: `bin/setup` (installs gems, yarn packages, prepares database).
+- Run app locally: `bundle exec rails s` (Web, http://anedot.me:3000), `bundle exec sidekiq` (jobs), `bin/webpack-dev-server` (hot reloading for frontend).
+- Database utilities: `bundle exec rails db:migrate` to apply migrations; `bundle exec rails db:reset` to rebuild from scratch.
+- API docs: `RAILS_ENV=test bundle exec rake rswag:specs:swaggerize` to regenerate Swagger; open with `yarn docs:open:mac` or `yarn docs:open:windows`.
+
+## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
+- Ruby: enforced by RuboCop (Rails + RSpec cops); 2-space indentation, ~100-char lines, snake_case file names, CamelCase classes/modules. Prefer service objects and POROs in `app/services` for new logic.
+- JS/React: ESLint + Prettier defaults; keep shared UI in `app/frontend/components`, feature-specific code under `app/frontend/namespaces/*`. Name hooks `useSomething`, components `PascalCase`, and tests `*.spec.js`/`*.test.js` near the code or under `jest/`.
+- Styles: follow existing SCSS/CSS modules and avoid introducing global selectors unless intentional.
+
+## Testing Guidelines
+- Backend: `bundle exec rspec`; keep fixtures/factories close to specs and favor request specs for API changes. Use `bundle exec rake database_consistency` when adjusting schemas.
+- Frontend: `yarn jest` (collects coverage by default); rely on Testing Library helpers set up in `jest/__setup__`. Mock network calls with `axios-mock-adapter` where possible.
+- New features should include happy-path and failure coverage; update swagger specs when API contracts change.
+
+## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
+- Commits: concise, imperative messages per Tim Pope’s guidance (e.g., “Fix donor validation”), grouped logically.
+- PR titles: prefix with a tag such as `[BUGFIX]`, `[FEATURE]`, `[INTERNAL]`, `[MAINTENANCE]`, or `[SECURITY]` followed by a short summary.
+- PR body: fill the template (summary, linked issues, screenshots for UI, test/QA checkboxes, cross-browser notes). Label appropriately (e.g., `needs review(s)`, `needs QA`) and request at least one approval. Rebase onto latest `master` before merging to avoid merge bubbles.
*** End Patch

module Api
  module Org
    module V3
      # v3 api endpoints for account_documents
      class AccountDocumentsController < BaseController
        before_action :set_document, only: %i[update destroy]

        def index
          authorize %i[api org v3 account_document]

          documents = @account.documents.page(page).per(per_page)

          serialize_collection(documents)
        end

        def create
          authorize %i[api org v3 account_document]

          document = @account.documents.new(
            account_documents_params.merge(uploaded_by: current_user)
          )

          if document.save
            serialize_obj(document, status: :created)
          else
            serialize_errors_for(document)
          end
        end

        def update
          authorize [:api, :org, :v3, @document]

          if @document.update(account_documents_params)
            serialize_obj(@document)
          else
            serialize_errors_for(@document)
          end
        end

        def destroy
          authorize [:api, :org, :v3, @document]

          @document.destroy
          head :no_content
        end

      private

        def account_documents_params
          params.require(params_wrapper).permit(
            *WRAPPED_MODEL_PARAMS.map(&:underscore)
          ).merge(account_id: @account.id)
        end

        def set_account
          @account = current_account_scope.find_by!(uid: params[:account_id])
        end

        def set_document
          @document = @account.documents.find(params[:id])
        end

        WRAPPED_MODEL_PARAMS = %w[
          accountId
          documentType
          documentName
          file
          id
        ].freeze

        wrap_parameters params_wrapper, include: WRAPPED_MODEL_PARAMS
      end
    end
  end
end

private

+# frozen_string_literal: true
+
+module AccountDocuments
+  # Uses Amazon Bedrock to validate that a document matches its claimed type.
+  class BedrockValidator
+    class ConfigurationError < StandardError; end
+    class ValidationError < StandardError; end
+
+    MAX_BASE64_LENGTH = 400_000
+    DEFAULT_MODEL_ID = ENV.fetch("BEDROCK_MODEL_ID", "anthropic.claude-3-haiku-20240307")
+    REGION = ENV.fetch("AWS_BEDROCK_REGION", "us-east-1")
+
+    def initialize(document:, claimed_type: nil, client: nil, model_id: DEFAULT_MODEL_ID)
+      @document = document
+      @claimed_type = claimed_type || document.document_type
+      @client = client || default_client
+      @model_id = model_id
+    end
+
+    def call
+      raise ConfigurationError, "Bedrock client is not configured" unless @client
+      raise ValidationError, "Document file missing" unless @document.file&.file
+
+      body = request_body.to_json
+      response = @client.invoke_model(
+        model_id: @model_id,
+        body: body,
+        content_type: "application/json",
+        accept: "application/json"
+      )
+
+      parsed = parse_response(response)
+
+      {
+        verdict: parsed.fetch("verdict"),
+        confidence: parsed.fetch("confidence"),
+        reason: parsed.fetch("reason"),
+        model_id: @model_id
+      }
+    rescue Aws::Errors::ServiceError => e
+      raise ValidationError, "Bedrock request failed: #{e.message}"
+    rescue JSON::ParserError => e
+      raise ValidationError, "Unable to parse Bedrock response: #{e.message}"
+    end
+
+  private
+
+    def default_client
+      credentials = if ENV["AWS_BEDROCK_ACCESS_KEY_ID"] && ENV["AWS_BEDROCK_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"]
+                      Aws::Credentials.new(
+                        ENV["AWS_BEDROCK_ACCESS_KEY_ID"],
+                        ENV["AWS_BEDROCK_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"]
+                      )
+                    end
+
+      Aws::BedrockRuntime::Client.new(region: REGION, credentials:)
+    rescue Aws::Errors::MissingRegionError
+      nil
+    end
+
+    def parse_response(response)
+      payload = response.body.respond_to?(:read) ? response.body.read : response.body
+      envelope = JSON.parse(payload)
+      content_text = extract_content_text(envelope)
+
+      JSON.parse(content_text)
+    end
+
+    def extract_content_text(envelope)
+      if envelope["content"].is_a?(Array)
+        text_chunk = envelope["content"].find { |c| c["type"] == "text" }&.fetch("text", nil)
+        return text_chunk if text_chunk.present?
+      end
+
+      return envelope["completion"] if envelope["completion"].present?
+
+      raise ValidationError, "Bedrock returned an unexpected payload"
+    end
+
+    def request_body
+      {
+        anthropic_version: "bedrock-2023-05-31",
+        max_tokens: 256,
+        temperature: 0,
+        system: system_prompt,
+        messages: [
+          {
+            role: "user",
+            content: [
+              { type: "text", text: user_prompt }
+            ]
+          }
+        ]
+      }
+    end
+
+    def system_prompt
+      <<~PROMPT.squish
+        You are a strict validator for compliance documents. Decode the provided base64 content,
+        scan the text, and decide if the document matches the claimed type from the allowed list.
+        Only respond with compact JSON: {"verdict":"match|mismatch|unsure","confidence":0-1,"reason":"short explanation"}.
+        Choose "match" only when the content clearly aligns with the claimed type; choose "mismatch"
+        when it clearly does not; otherwise use "unsure". Do not include any extra text or code fences.
+      PROMPT
+    end
+
+    def user_prompt
+      <<~PROMPT
+        Claimed document type: "#{@claimed_type}"
+        Allowed document types: #{AccountDocument::DOCUMENT_TYPES.join(", ")}

**Clarifying CSRF token requirements**

The API requires both the session cookie and an `X-CSRF-Token` header for POST requests; to test in Postman, grab the `_anedot_session` cookie and the CSRF token from a logged-in browser’s meta tag or initial HTML response, then include both in requests with `Content-Type: application/json`.

summary.text:
**Clarifying CSRF token requirements**

The API requires both the session cookie and an `X-CSRF-Token` header for POST requests; to test in Postman, grab the `_anedot_session` cookie and the CSRF token from a logged-in browser’s meta tag or initial HTML response, then include both in requests with `Content-Type: application/json`.

payload.message:
`AccountDocumentsController` inherits the API base controller, which still enforces Rails’ CSRF protection. For POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE you must send both the session cookie and an `X-CSRF-Token` header. If either is missing/invalid, you’ll get “Can’t verify CSRF token authenticity.”

Quick way to get what Postman needs:
1) In a browser where you’re already logged in locally, open DevTools → Network on any API call. Copy the `_anedot_session` (and any other auth cookies) and the `X-CSRF-Token` header value from that request.
2) In Postman, set a `Cookie` header with that session cookie, and set `X-CSRF-Token` to the copied token. Keep `Content-Type: application/json`.

Bruno doesn’t have a special “auth” type for this; you need to reuse your Rails session + CSRF token. Easiest path:

**Clarifying 406 cause and fix**

The 406 error happens because the request defaults to HTML format, which the API rejects via `reject_html_requests`. To fix this, clients must include `Accept: application/json` and provide `account_id` as a query param (not just in body), matching parameter naming (`account_id` underscore). I'll explain this clearly with a curl example showing correct headers and query usage.

**Clarifying 406 cause and fix**

The 406 error happens because the request defaults to HTML format, which the API rejects via `reject_html_requests`. To fix this, clients must include `Accept: application/json` and provide `account_id` as a query param (not just in body), matching parameter naming (`account_id` underscore). payload.message:
A 406 here usually means the request is being treated as HTML (so `reject_html_requests` fires) and the params aren’t getting parsed. Two things to fix:

1) Send JSON headers so the request isn’t HTML:
- `Accept: application/json`
- `Content-Type: application/json`

2) Make sure `account_id` is present (snake_case) on the request, e.g. in the query string:
```
POST http://anedot.me:3000/org/v3/account_documents/123/validate_type?account_id=a59ba981aa5cfdfee337a
```
(or in the JSON body as `"account_id": "a59ba981aa5cfdfee337a"`).

Complete example (adjust ids/cookies/token):
```
curl -X POST \
  'http://anedot.me:3000/org/v3/account_documents/123/validate_type?account_id=a59ba981aa5cfdfee337a' \
  -H 'Cookie: _anedot_session_development=...' \
  -H 'X-CSRF-Token: ...' \
  -H 'Accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{ "documentType": "irs_w9" }'
```

In Bruno, add the two headers above and ensure `account_id` is either on the URL query or as a JSON field (snake_case).

summary.text:
**Recommending cookie jar with separate session**

Suggest using a cookie jar that accepts Set-Cookie updates (e.g., Bruno with cookie jar enabled) and performing API requests from a separate browser session—like an incognito tab—to capture a stable session cookie and CSRF token without front-end interference that causes rotation.

payload.message:
That rotation is coming from the UI polling in the same browser session. Easiest workaround: create a clean session that the UI isn’t touching, and reuse it in Bruno.

Practical steps:
1) Open a private/incognito window and log in to http://anedot.me:3000 once. Don’t load the main app in a tab that polls; just stay on a static page (or close the tab after login if your session persists).
2) From that incognito window, grab:
   - Cookie `_anedot_session_development` (Application → Cookies).
   - CSRF token: `document.querySelector('meta[name=csrf-token]').content`.
3) In Bruno, enable its cookie jar so it will accept any `Set-Cookie` updates. Seed the jar with `_anedot_session_development=<value>` for domain `anedot.me`.
4) Add headers: `X-CSRF-Token: <value>`, `Accept: application/json`, `Content-Type: application/json`.
5) Call your endpoint.

Because the UI isn’t polling in that incognito session, the cookie/token will stay stable. If you do need to refresh, repeat the incognito login and re-copy both values.

**Outlining curl-based local auth approach**

I'm planning a programmatic signin using curl: first fetch the login page to grab the CSRF token, then POST credentials with the token and stored cookies to get a session cookie for reuse in API calls. I'll provide example commands and note alternatives like personal tokens if supported.

summary.text:
**Outlining curl-based local auth approach**

I'm planning a programmatic signin using curl: first fetch the login page to grab the CSRF token, then POST credentials with the token and stored cookies to get a session cookie for reuse in API calls. I'll provide example commands and note alternatives like personal tokens if supported.

payload.message:
You can get a stable session/CSRF pair without loading the frontend by logging in once via curl and reusing the saved cookie jar in Bruno. Steps (dev):

1) Get the login page and CSRF token (stores the session cookie in `cookies.txt`):
```bash
curl -c cookies.txt -s http://anedot.me:3000/users/sign_in -o /tmp/login.html
csrf=$(ruby -e 'puts File.read("/tmp/login.html")[/name="csrf-token" content="([^"]+)"/,1]')
```

2) Post the login form with your credentials (reuses/updates the same cookie jar):
```bash
curl -b cookies.txt -c cookies.txt \
  -H "X-CSRF-Token: $csrf" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -X POST \
  -d "user[email]=you@example.com&user[password]=your_password" \
  http://anedot.me:3000/users/sign_in
```

After the 200/302, `cookies.txt` will contain `_anedot_session_development` tied to that CSRF token.

3) In Bruno, import/use that cookie jar (`cookies.txt`) and set headers:
- `X-CSRF-Token: <same $csrf value from step 1>`
- `Accept: application/json`
- `Content-Type: application/json`

4) Call the endpoint:
```
POST http://anedot.me:3000/org/v3/account_documents/:id/validate_type?account_id=...
```
Body optional: `{ "documentType": "irs_w9" }`.

Because the session was created without running the polling UI, the cookie won’t rotate. If you restart the server or log out, just repeat the curl steps to refresh the jar and token.
